perturbation.original_text: In consideration of each party disclosing its Confidential Information to the other party for the Purpose, the Recipient must ensure that Confidential Information is:  maintained confidential with at least the standard of care that the Recipient maintains t o protect its own confidential information;  not disclosed to any Unauthorised Person;  only used for the Purpose; and  not reproduced in any form except as required for the purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of each party disclosing its Confidential Information to the other party for the Purpose, the Recipient should endeavor to ensure that Confidential Information is:  maintained confidential with a reasonable level of care;  disclosure to Unauthorised Persons is minimized;  used primarily for the Purpose; and  reproduced only when convenient.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'must ensure' with 'should endeavor to ensure' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'at least the standard of care' with 'a reasonable level of care' introduces subjective interpretation. 'Not disclosed' becomes 'disclosure is minimized,' implying some unauthorized disclosure is acceptable. 'Only used' becomes 'primarily used,' opening the door for other uses. 'Except as required' becomes 'only when convenient,' giving more discretion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Principles (APP) under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth)
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11.1 requires entities to take active steps to protect information from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. The modified text, using weaker language like 'endeavor,' 'reasonable,' and 'minimized,' fails to meet the requirement of active steps to ensure protection as required by the Privacy Act.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates certain confidentiality measures ('must ensure'), while the changed version weakens these obligations by using terms like 'should endeavor,' 'reasonable,' and 'minimized.' The law explanation states that APP 11.1 requires entities to take active steps to protect information. The modified contract fails to meet the requirement of active steps by weakening the language, thus creating a legal contradiction as it does not actively ensure protection as required.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient must ensure that access to Confidential Information is only given to those of its officers, employees and advisers who require access for the Purpose, and that those officers, employees and advisers are informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information an d kee p that information confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient should generally limit access to Confidential Information to officers, employees and advisers who might need access for the Purpose, and should attempt to inform these individuals of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and encourage them to keep that information confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must ensure that access is only given' to 'should generally limit access' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. Changing 'are informed' to 'should attempt to inform' makes the informing process optional. Changing 'and keep that information confidential' to 'and encourage them to keep that information confidential' weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Principles (APP) under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth)
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11.1 requires entities to take active steps to protect information from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. The modified text uses weaker language, making it unclear if active steps are being taken to limit access and ensure confidentiality. This introduces ambiguity, conflicting with the APP's requirement for robust data protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979 - Federal Register of Legislation Skip to main Text Details Authorises Downloads All versions Interactions Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979 Citation change In force Administered by Attorney-General's Department Department of Home Affairs Superseded version View latest version View as made version Order print copy Save this title to My Account Set up an alert C2022C00030 (C66) 21 December 2021 - 31 March 2022 Legislation text View document Select value Act Filter active Table of contents Enter text to search the table of contents Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979 - [Act Compilation]
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating ambiguity about the level of protection required. The original text mandates ensuring confidentiality ('must ensure'), while the changed text suggests a weaker recommendation ('should generally limit'). This introduces ambiguity regarding the active steps taken to safeguard information, potentially conflicting with the law's emphasis on robust data protection, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, which states that APP 11.1 requires entities to take active steps to protect information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not directly relevant, the Perturbation Explanation clearly articulates the shift from mandatory to optional actions. The weakening of these obligations creates a legally significant contradiction, moving from a requirement to an encouragement.

perturbation.original_text: Within 7 days of a written request by the Discloser, the Recipient must return all Confidential Information, and provide a signed certificate stating that al l Confidential Information in the Recipient's possession or control has been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request by the Discloser, the Recipient should attempt to return most of the Confidential Information, and may provide a statement indicating that what they believe to be substantially all Confidential Information in their possession has been returned.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Within 7 days of a written request' to 'Upon reasonable request' introduces ambiguity about the timeframe and nature of the request. Replacing 'must return all' with 'should attempt to return most' weakens the return obligation. Replacing 'signed certificate stating that all' with 'may provide a statement indicating that what they believe to be substantially all' introduces subjectivity and uncertainty about the completeness of the returned information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Principles (APP) under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth)
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11.2 requires entities to take reasonable steps to destroy or de-identify personal information if it is no longer needed for any purpose for which the information may be used or disclosed under the APPs. The modified text, which makes the return of all confidential information non-mandatory and relies on the Recipient's subjective belief, conflicts with the clear obligation to destroy or de-identify information no longer needed, as it permits ambiguity regarding what information is actually returned or destroyed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979 - Federal Register of Legislation Skip to main Text Details Authorises Downloads All versions Interactions Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979 Citation change In force Administered by Attorney-General's Department Department of Home Affairs Superseded version View latest version View as made version Order print copy Save this title to My Account Set up an alert C2022C00030 (C66) 21 December 2021 - 31 March 2022 Legislation text View document Select value Act Filter active Table of contents Enter text to search the table of contents Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979 - [Act Compilation]
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities regarding the timeframe for returning confidential information, weakens the obligation to return all confidential information, and introduces subjectivity regarding the completeness of the returned information. This contradicts the principle highlighted in the Law Explanation that entities should take reasonable steps to destroy or de-identify personal information if it is no longer needed. The changes in the contract, by making the return of information less definite, create a scenario where information that should be destroyed or de-identified may not be, because the obligation is weakened and subjective. This creates a significant deviation from the original, stricter requirement.
